question_patterns: give me a beginner gym day
intro: Here is a simple beginner gym day that stays repeatable.
guidance: Pick 3 to 4 movements, use 2 to 3 controlled sets each, and leave the session feeling repeatable.
Use light to moderate load at first, keep reps smooth, and stop before form slips.
caution: Consistency matters more than maxing out in the first week; increase load only after the setup and movement feel steady.

question_patterns: what should i do for upper body
intro: These are solid upper-body picks from your current workout data.
guidance: Use one row, one press, and one or two arm, shoulder, or core accessories.
Start with controlled sets in the 8 to 12 rep range and keep shoulder position relaxed instead of shrugged.
caution: If a machine setup feels wrong, fix seat, pad, handle, and joint alignment before adding load.

question_patterns: what should i do for lower body
intro: These are strong lower-body options from your current workout data.
guidance: Build around leg press, leg curl, leg extension, and one glute, calf, or core accessory.
Use controlled reps, keep the knees tracking comfortably, and line your joints up with the machine path.
caution: If knees or hips feel pressured, reduce load, revisit setup, and use a controlled pain-free range.

question_patterns: what cardio should i do today
intro: These are practical cardio directions you can use today.
guidance: Pick one repeatable machine and keep the effort steady unless intervals are the specific goal.
Walking, cycling, stepper work, and elliptical sessions all fit well when the pace can be repeated across the week.
caution: Cardio works best as a weekly habit pattern instead of one all-out session followed by long gaps.

question_patterns: what machines should a beginner use
intro: These are sensible beginner-friendly machine directions.
guidance: Start with stable machines such as leg press, chest press, seated row, leg curl, and simple cardio machines.
Practice setup and controlled reps before chasing heavier loads.
caution: A machine is only beginner-friendly if the seat, pads, and starting position can be set comfortably.

question_patterns: what should i do if im tired
intro: On a tired day, use a lower-pressure session instead of forcing a maximal workout.
guidance: Choose easy cardio, lighter machine work, or fewer total sets.
Keep the goal as movement quality and consistency, not personal records.
caution: If tiredness comes with dizziness, chest pain, fainting, illness, or unusual symptoms, skip the workout and get appropriate guidance.

intro: Increase weight only after the current load feels controlled and repeatable.
guidance: Add load when you can complete the target reps with clean form, stable setup, and no joint discomfort.
Use small increases and keep one or two reps in reserve instead of jumping straight to a hard set.
caution: If form breaks, range shortens, or joints take over, stay at the current load or reduce it.

question_patterns: how many sets should i do
how many reps should i do
what rep range should i use
intro: For a simple starting point, use 2 to 3 controlled sets per movement.
guidance: Most machine strength work can start around 8 to 12 controlled reps.
For beginners, clean setup and repeatable form matter more than perfect rep math.
caution: Stop the set before form slips or joint pressure builds.

intro: Mild muscle soreness can often be handled with easier movement, but sharp pain is different.
guidance: Use light cardio, mobility, or train a less-sore area if movement feels normal.
Avoid heavy loading the same sore area if range of motion or control is clearly worse.
caution: Sharp, worsening, one-sided, or joint-based pain should not be treated as normal soreness.

intro: Most cardio sessions should feel repeatable rather than all-out.
guidance: Use a pace where breathing is elevated but controlled for steady sessions.
Save harder intervals for days when recovery, joints, and energy are good.
caution: Chest pain, fainting, severe dizziness, or unusual breathlessness needs medical guidance.
